9.text: This lab keeps the same algorithm as pure Python, but swaps scalar arithmetic for arrays. The goal is speed without surrendering understanding of the gradients.

10.text: Same dataset, same task. That makes the comparison with lab 01 about representation and backprop mechanics, not about a different problem.

11.text: We keep the data pipeline close to lab 01 so the reader can focus on the real shift: vectorized forward pass, hand-derived backward pass.

13.text: Here is the big transition from lab 01: NumPy compresses the forward pass into matrix operations, but we still refuse to hide the gradient story behind autograd.

15.text: Use the same reproducible setup as lab 01 so the new complexity comes from arrays and manual gradients, not from a different run.

16.text: NumPy has its own RNG because arrays bring their own initialization path. That separation is the first hint that frameworks often manage randomness differently too.

21.text: Same shared corpus as lab 01. That continuity is what lets the framework progression feel like one unfolding story instead of separate demos.

22.text: Keep the tutorial self-contained here too so the only new burden is understanding the math, not setting up the environment.

25.text: Download URL for the names dataset.

28.text: Same tokenization as lab 01, each line becomes one training document.

33.text: BOS token ID, same convention across all labs.

40.text: Same tiny architecture as lab 01, this lab is about the computation method, not the model size.

42.text: A single transformer layer keeps the model minimal. Real GPTs stack dozens, but one is enough to learn interesting name patterns and demonstrate the full algorithm.

47.text: Helper to create a random NumPy array, replaces lab 01's nested list of Value objects. One line, same effect.

48.text: Standard normal scaled by 0.08, same initialization distribution as lab 01.

52.text: Parameters stored as a flat dict of NumPy arrays. Compare to lab 01's nested lists of Value objects, same data, vastly more efficient storage.

53.text: Token embeddings: each row is one token's learned representation.

54.text: Position embeddings: each row encodes a position in the sequence.

55.text: Output projection: maps embeddings back to vocabulary scores.

57.text: Allocate per-layer attention and MLP weights.

58.text: The query weight matrix. Multiplying the input by this produces the 'what am I looking for?' vectors for attention.

59.text: The key weight matrix. It produces 'what do I contain?' vectors that queries will be matched against to determine relevance.

60.text: The value weight matrix. It produces vectors carrying the actual content that attention will retrieve and blend together.

61.text: Output projection recombines multi-head attention outputs. After each head focuses on different relationships, this matrix merges their perspectives into a unified representation.

66.text: Every parameter gets a matching zero-filled gradient array. In lab 01, gradients lived inside each Value. Here, we manage them ourselves.

68.text: Count total parameters to verify we match lab 01.

75.text: RMSNorm forward, returns both the output AND a cache of intermediate values. The backward pass needs these cached values to compute gradients.

77.text: Mean of squares across the embedding dimension. NumPy handles this across entire matrices, not one scalar at a time.

78.text: Reciprocal square root, the scaling factor. Same formula as lab 01's (ms + 1e-5) ** -0.5.

79.text: Scale the input. Broadcasting handles the (T, 1) * (T, D) multiplication automatically.

80.text: Return the output AND the cache. This is the key pattern: forward stores everything backward will need.

83.text: RMSNorm backward, given the gradient of the output, compute the gradient of the input. This is the manual derivative you'd get from the chain rule on paper.

84.text: Unpack the cached values from the forward pass.

86.text: The hand-derived RMSNorm gradient. Two terms: direct path through the scale, and indirect path through the normalization. This is what autograd computes automatically.

90.text: Softmax forward, same subtract-max-then-exponentiate trick as lab 01, but vectorized over entire matrices.

92.text: Subtract max for numerical stability, prevents overflow in exp().

94.text: Normalize to get probabilities. Each row sums to 1.

97.text: Softmax backward. The Jacobian of softmax is dense (every output depends on every input), but this compact formula handles it.

99.text: Sum of element-wise product: this captures the coupling between all softmax outputs.

100.text: Elegant formula: p * (dp - sum(dp * p)). Derived from differentiating the softmax function, doing this by hand is a rite of passage.

103.text: ReLU forward: save a binary mask of which neurons fired. The backward pass just uses this mask as a gate.

104.text: The mask is 1 where input was positive, 0 elsewhere. This IS the ReLU derivative.

105.text: Element-wise multiply: zero out negative inputs.

109.text: ReLU backward: gradients pass through where the neuron was active, get blocked where it was dead. Multiply by the mask, that's it.

111.text: Dead simple: dout * mask. If the neuron fired in the forward pass, its gradient flows; if not, it's zero.

114.text: The forward pass processes an entire sequence at once as a matrix, no more position-by-position loop like lab 01.

119.text: How many tokens we're processing. The model will see all of these at once, unlike lab 01 which processed one position at a time.

120.text: The cache dictionary stores every intermediate result the backward pass will need.

123.text: Gather all token embeddings at once with fancy indexing. In lab 01, this was a lookup in a loop.

125.text: Broadcast addition fuses token and position information, same as lab 01 but for all positions simultaneously.

128.text: Normalize and cache. Every intermediate result stored here will be consumed during backpropagation.

131.text: Snapshot the representation before attention transforms it. The residual connection will add this back, ensuring the layer can only refine, not destroy, what came before.

134.text: Normalize and cache before attention. The cache stores the normalization statistics so the backward pass can compute exact gradients without re-doing the forward math.

137.text: Query projection: (n, D) @ (D, D). One matrix multiply replaces lab 01's nested list-comprehension linear layer.

139.text: Project into value vectors, which carry the actual information that attention will retrieve and blend. Same operation as Q and K, different learned weights.

142.text: Reshape and transpose to split into attention heads. The (n, D) matrix becomes (n_head, n, head_dim), each head gets its own slice.

143.text: Keys get the same multi-head split. Each head's keys will be matched against only that head's queries, so different heads learn different notions of relevance.

144.text: Values get the same multi-head split. Each head independently retrieves content from its own subspace, then the results are concatenated.

147.text: Batch matrix multiply: all heads compute attention scores simultaneously. One operation replaces lab 01's per-head loop.

149.text: Causal mask: upper triangle of ones. Prevents tokens from attending to future positions, the core constraint of autoregressive generation.

150.text: Fill future positions with -1e9 so softmax gives them ~zero probability.

151.text: Softmax over the last dimension, each row becomes a probability distribution over which past tokens to attend to.

154.text: Weighted sum of values using attention probabilities, the actual information retrieval step.

156.text: Reverse the reshape: merge heads back into a single (n, D) matrix.

159.text: Output projection: mix the multi-head outputs.

162.text: Residual connection: add attention output to original input.

164.text: Cache EVERYTHING the attention backward pass needs. Eleven values, this is the price of manual backprop.

167.text: Snapshot the representation before the MLP transforms it. The residual connection will add this back, letting gradients flow through an unobstructed shortcut path.

168.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network. Like the pre-attention norm, this keeps activations on a consistent scale so the MLP can learn stable transformations.

171.text: MLP expansion: project to 4x wider hidden layer.

172.text: ReLU activation with its binary mask cached for the backward pass. The mask records which neurons fired, which is all the backward pass needs to route gradients.

173.text: MLP contraction: project back to embedding dimension.

175.text: Add the MLP output back to its input via the residual connection. This is the second skip connection in the block, completing the norm-transform-add pattern.

176.text: Cache all MLP intermediates for backward.

179.text: Final linear projection to vocabulary logits. Note the transpose on lm_head, weight tying convention.

180.text: Cache the final hidden states for the backward pass.

184.text: Combines forward pass with loss computation, the complete training forward step.

186.text: Total sequence length before clipping. We need this to determine how many input-target pairs we can form from this document.

187.text: Clip to the maximum context window. Most names are short enough to fit, but this guard prevents index-out-of-bounds on unusually long entries.

189.text: Target tokens (all but first), shifted by one position.

191.text: Feed the tokens through the model to get logits and the cache of intermediates. The cache is what makes manual backprop possible without recomputing the forward pass.

194.text: Convert logits to probabilities for cross-entropy loss.

195.text: Gather the log-probabilities of the correct targets. The 1e-10 prevents log(0).

196.text: Mean negative log-probability, cross-entropy loss. Lower means the model assigns higher probability to the right answers.

198.text: Pack everything into the cache for the backward pass.

202.text: The backward pass: 80+ lines of hand-derived gradients. This is what loss.backward() does automatically in PyTorch.

207.text: Softmax + cross-entropy gradient shortcut: start with the probabilities, subtract 1 from the correct class. This elegant shorthand replaces two separate backward passes.

208.text: Subtract 1 at target positions, the combined softmax-CE gradient.

209.text: Average over the sequence, matches the mean in the loss computation.

212.text: Gradient for lm_head: dlogits.T @ x. Accumulate with += because gradients add up.

213.text: Gradient flowing back into the hidden states.

215.text: Walk backward through layers, reverse order of the forward pass.

217.text: Unpack the four intermediate values the MLP forward pass cached. Each one is needed to compute the exact gradient for the corresponding weight matrix.

219.text: Save residual gradient, it bypasses the MLP and adds directly later.

221.text: Gradient through fc2: the backward of act @ fc2 w.r.t. act.

222.text: Weight gradient for fc2: act.T @ dx. This is the outer product pattern: input.T @ output_grad.

224.text: ReLU backward: zero out gradients for dead neurons.

227.text: Gradient through fc1: same pattern as fc2.

228.text: Weight gradient for fc1: the outer product of the normalized input and the hidden gradient. This tells us how to adjust fc1 to reduce the loss.

231.text: RMSNorm backward, uses the cached normalization statistics.

232.text: Add the residual gradient. The residual connection means gradients take two paths, through the MLP and around it, and they add together.

235.text: Unpack the attention cache, all eleven intermediate values.

237.text: Save the residual gradient for the attention skip connection.

241.text: Weight gradient for the output projection.

244.text: Reshape gradient back into multi-head format.

247.text: Gradient through att_probs @ V_h w.r.t. attention probabilities.

248.text: Gradient flowing back into value vectors. The attention probabilities determine how much each value contributed to the output, so transposing them gives the correct routing.

251.text: Softmax backward on attention weights, uses the formula from softmax_bwd.

253.text: Zero out gradients for masked (future) positions, they were -inf in the forward pass.

255.text: Undo the sqrt(head_dim) scaling from the forward pass.

262.text: Reshape head gradients back to (n, D).

263.text: Key gradients get the same reshape back to full embedding dimension. The per-head gradients are simply concatenated since the heads operate on non-overlapping slices.

264.text: Value gradients reshaped identically. All three QKV gradients flow back through the same reshape-transpose pipeline that split them into heads during the forward pass.

267.text: Sum gradients from all three QKV projections. The same x_normed feeds into Q, K, and V, so its gradient is the sum of all three paths.

273.text: RMSNorm backward for the pre-attention norm.

274.text: Add residual gradient, completing the attention skip connection.

277.text: Initial RMSNorm backward, the very first normalization in the forward pass.

280.text: Embedding gradients: scatter the position-level gradients back to the embedding tables. Unlike weight matrices, embeddings use row indexing, so we accumulate manually.

281.text: Each token ID's embedding row gets the gradient from that position.

282.text: Each position's embedding row gets the same gradient.

289.text: First moment buffers, matching dict structure to parameters.

290.text: Second moment buffers. Named Vb to avoid shadowing Python's v loop variable.

296.text: The main training loop. Each iteration: pick a name, forward pass, manual backward pass, Adam update. One thousand steps through this cycle.

297.text: Cycle through the dataset with modular indexing. After seeing all names, we loop back to the beginning for another pass.

299.text: Convert to NumPy array for the forward pass.

302.text: Zero all gradients before each step. In lab 01, this was p.grad = 0 per parameter; here it's per array.

303.text: Fill with zeros, faster than creating new arrays.

306.text: Backward pass, our 80+ lines of hand-coded gradients. In lab 03, this will be one line.

309.text: Linear learning rate decay, same schedule as lab 01.

310.text: Iterate over every parameter array in the model to apply the Adam update. NumPy broadcasting handles entire weight matrices at once.

311.text: First moment update, exponential moving average of gradients.

312.text: Second moment update, exponential moving average of squared gradients.

315.text: Adam parameter update. Same formula as lab 01, but operating on entire arrays at once thanks to NumPy broadcasting.

317.text: Log every 10 steps (lab 01 logged every step, the NumPy version is fast enough that would be too verbose).

327.text: Generate 20 entirely new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution. These names never appeared in the training data.

328.text: Start with BOS token in the generated sequence.

331.text: Run the forward pass on the entire generated sequence so far. Unlike lab 01's KV-cache approach, we recompute from scratch each step.

332.text: Take only the last position's logits and apply temperature scaling.

333.text: Reshape for softmax (expects 2D input) and get probabilities.

334.text: Sample from the distribution using NumPy's RNG.

335.text: When the model outputs the BOS token during generation, it is signaling that this name is complete and we should stop adding characters.

337.text: Extend the sequence with the new token.

339.text: Print the finished name. These are entirely new names invented by the model, reflecting the statistical patterns it absorbed from 32K real names.
